text: The surgeon places four 8mm robotic trocars in the left quadrant and a 12mm assistant trocar in the right flank.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels and makes an incision in the peritoneum, opening the space between the rota and total fascia. Dissection proceeds through the vascular plane in a medial to lateral direction.
The left iliac artery and left ureter are visible, covered by the Girota fascia, within the surgical field.
The surgeon skeletonizes the ileocolic vessels at their origin from the superior mesenteric vessels, carefully exposing these vascular structures.
The surgeon clips and divides the ileocolic vein, followed by applying clips on the ileocolic artery and dividing the vessel.
The duodenum, pancreas, and Fredae fascia are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform precise dissection of the anatomical plane, carefully separating tissue layers within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the last ileal loop and proceeds to coagulate the arched tracts using electrocautery.
The surgeon staples the last ileal loop, securing it in place as part of the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon clips and divides the right branch of the mid-colic artery, which is positioned on the left side in this case.
The surgeon sections the fomental vessels, achieving complete mobilization of the specimen, with visible anatomical structures such as the duodenum, pancreas, and fredae fascia.
The surgeon staples the transverse colon, securing the tissue with surgical staples.
The robotic platform is repositioned to facilitate the anastomosis without interference. A 3-0 silk stay suture is placed to ensure proper orientation of the bowel segments.

A two-centimeter opening is created in the transverse colon and small bowel. The surgeon performs a robotic hand-sewn anastomosis using a single-layer barbed-running suture to connect the two structures.
Silk sutures are placed to prevent traction on the anastomosis and to close the mesenteric defect. The greater omentum is positioned over the anastomosis for coverage.
